sentences.text: Well, by then, my father had his own factory, an incandescent lamp factory.
They made all kinds of shapes and colors.
And he had some very, very big contracts, like the theaters, some of the theaters in Vienna.
We'll come back to the theater later.

Some of the theaters in Vienna, he had a contract with that whatever lights and bulbs and spotlights and whatever a theater needs, we'll supply for a fixed amount per year, whatever term the contract ran.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course sometimes we got passes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so it a very sizable-- I mean, it wasn't the General Electric or anything, but it was a pretty important part of our lives, at least, but also of the Austrian economy.
We want to say that.
And so he came back to his office on the next trolley, I suppose.
It was too far to walk.
And this went on-- Hitler came in March.
This went on for a few weeks, and these were some harrowing weeks because so many people who owed had left and those who hadn't left didn't pay because it was a Jewish firm.
And we had-- of course, we had representatives who acquired business.
And one of them, who was always very, very courteous and a very nice, gentlemen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] decided that as long as my father couldn't keep the place, why didn't he take over?
At least we're on friendly terms, and he's not going to hurt anybody.
And why should he always have to use his shoe leather to acquire customers if you could sit at the office and just pick up the phone?
That's what he thought an owner or a boss does.
And so he, being of pure Aryan descent, had no problem, went to the party and they put him in as an-- an Aryan manager-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], the term was-- an Aryan manager.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Hitler came middle of March and the beginning of April, so a couple weeks, three weeks later, a very important looking uniformed guy came in and said they wanted to take my father for a for a deposition.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I was working in the office, not in-- not physically in my father's office, but my father's firm.
And my father came to the office where I was working and said, I have to make a deposition.
And this man who took him for the deposition said, and don't you worry.
He'll be home for dinner, or something like that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, well he came home about a year later.
He was taken into protective custody, and in a strange way, this too was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] call me stupid.
I always look for the silver lining.

This was a lifesaver, because he was taken to protective custody, and since they were running out of schools and police stations and other places where they put these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people that they just grabbed off the street or wherever, they put him into the regular jail with some very interesting burglars and robbers and criminals-- not violent criminals.
And since he never smoked, and they cigarette rations, he could exchange the cigarette ration for a little more food.
And since he was such a fine gentlemen, they all were very impressed with the nice gentleman they had among them now.
And they were surprised that he ate beans.
I don't know, they probably thought the upper classes of the Jews ate caviar.

And he had-- aside from not knowing what happens tomorrow and what happens to his family and-- and-- and then everything was confiscated, and how are we going to meet the expenses, but aside from that, he had a good time, because they tried to teach him how to burglarize, because you're a Jew, you don't know from nothing, and you're going out into a foreign country, and you don't know the language.
What are you going to do?
So they tried to teach him how to attack a lock and how to, you know, survive.
He told us these stories afterwards.
And they had a-- an intercom, because when you do certain things to the toilet, and the people on the lower floor, on the upper floor-- you know, you have sort of a Morse code with the flushes.
And they taught him all that, and he never made any use of it.
[LAUGHS] And this is not so funny.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, that's what happened to my father.
My mother and I stayed in the-- that was one floor of a two-family villa.
And so we stayed there for a while and then we got-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no, not yet.

We stayed there for over a year, because when my father was finally released, with nothing pending against him, the firm was gone by then, because this nice [SPEAKING GERMAN] thought the boss sits there, has a mistress, makes phone calls, and takes money.
And so all the money was exhausted.
Much of the outstanding money was never brought in.
He didn't have the faintest idea of production or distribution or business as such.
His wife divorced him because of the mistress.
And even though he had a car now, it just didn't work out.
And so the-- the party said, the man mismanaged and now the firm is kaput.
And he's responsible, because the Jew was gone.
It was under his leadership.
So they put him in jail.
It was the same jail where my father was.

And he went to trial and asked my-- asked my father-- or the lawyer got together-- to have my father give him-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what would you call that-- to talk about his character and how capable he was-- a character reference.
And my father was taken out his jail for just that trial and taken into-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] into-- into the-- the court and gave a very good report.
This man was always a hard worker.
I mean, this had gone to his head, and unfortunately the firm was no longer in existence.
He just miscalculated.
But he used to be a very decent man, but given the opportunity, he just went crazy with the sense of power and-- and [INAUDIBLE] didn't realize.
I think he got two or three years in jail, and my father was brought back into his cell.
But he never-- it never came to any trial or anything.
We had visitation rights.
My mother and I could see him once a week.
And when they-- when we were there, they told the guard, or whatever the person is in the jail, that the visitors are here, to bring the prisoner.
But he was really not a prisoner.
He was just put in holding there.
And they called him the white raven.
The weisse Rab sollt kommen.
The white raven should come, because there's nothing against him.
So he was known as the white raven.
It didn't help anybody, anything.

But when the Kristallnacht came-- and this is where I said that is the silver lining-- we didn't have to worry, because he was already, A, in jail, and B, not as a Jew and not in a Jewish holding facility or anything.
He was just plain in jail, among all his fellow Aryan jailmates, and so forth.
So he was safe.
And this was a horrible thing, this Kristallnacht.
It got the name much later.
I mean, We never realize that this was called Kristallnacht.
Yes, I--
And do you remember other episodes of Nazi cruelty?
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Not that that started with the Kristallnacht.
Of course, we had no phone anymore.
We did still live in that part of the house.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had taken a lawyer to see what goes on with my father.
And I was supposed to call the lawyer, and since we had no phone in the house anymore, I went down the street to the public phone, which was a block away and at the corner.

And I made my phone call and called the lawyer, came out of this phone booth, and two brown shirts-- the SA-- two brown shirts were there and tried to arrest me because I am a spy and I have given information to-- in French-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to a foreign newspaper.
I was flabbergasted.
I called Dr.
[GERMAN], please, call him back, and he'll tell you that we just spoke.
I don't even know French.
What-- what-- what information could I have?
There is no information to give-- nothing.
Nothing much happened today that's different from yesterday.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they realized-- I don't know if they realized, or it was they just got bored with me.
You'll never know what happens.
And after a few threats, they let me go.
So I went around the block to go home, still not knowing what went on.
This was in the morning.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I saw a group of school children being brought around the other corner into the street-- the teacher in front, the children two by two, and the teacher in the rear-- oh, I don't know, 40, 50 kids.
Then they were stopped in the middle of the block.
Now, I hadn't even known that in the middle of the block was a-- a little synagogue, a temple, a shul, a beitstube.
I don't know what it was.
They opened the doors and poured out the seats and pews and Torahs [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and set them on fire.
And that's what they brought the children for, so the children could stand on the sidewalk and see that in the front yard of that building, religious objects were being burned.
When I saw this, I turned the other corner around the block and ran home.
And then I realized, this is major.
And that's why they thought, you know, information.
Obviously, this was not a-- a singular [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] accidental incident, because the school children were already paraded there before it really burned.
I went home and asked my mother, please, close the door and don't go any place, and I'll stay here.
And-- and then later on, we had-- really, we didn't hear until evening, you know, that they had plundered and thrown in the windows and whatnot.
We lived in a residential section, so nothing much happened out there, until or unless a specific incident took place.
Now, that brings up a specific incident.
We [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lived on Billrothstrasse, which is a fairly large [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] main street in the suburbs.
I mean on the outskirts.
Of course, it still was in the city limits, towards the Vienna woods.
And there are two trolley lines going through that-- the 38 and the 39.
And a few blocks beyond the house where we lived, they separate.
The 38 goes here, and the 39 goes there.
They all go to the-- basically, to the Vienna woods, but different-- have the terminal at different places, not far from there.
So in between, is a triangular island.
And on one side, we had a piece of property that my parents had hoped to build their retirement home on in years to come.
My father was 50.
My mother was 47 when all this happened.
So that was far in the future, but we had a little weekend house there.

And I was walking from where we still lived to that weekend house, passed that island, and saw [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a bunch of hoodlums-- three or four-- attacking an old Jew-- a bearded Jew-- and pulling his beard out so that the blood ran over his chin with the flesh.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This I'll never forget.
[SIGHING] Actually, they sent in-- don't forget, Hitler came to power in '33.
We are talking of '38 to '39.
And they sent in German Nazis to learn from the Austrians how to be radical because they had gotten lax already.
They were in power for quite a while, and they were not as, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say brutal.
I don't know what use-- what they used.

And this is one of the reasons that some of the Austrian, old Nazis, who had gone through the times that the Nationalist Socialist Democratic Party was prohibited in Austria-- underground, fifth column-- were terribly disappointed after the Anschluss, because they expected to be rewarded.
And in came the German Nazis and took the top positions, and the Austrians were still the underlings.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I could tell you something about that-- I mentioned that we lived in a villa where the land-- the owner, lived on the first floor-- first floor in Vienna meant one flight up.
And we lived on the second floor.
The-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] owner had bought that villa from the estate of-- of some defunct noble family-- you know, they needed the money, whatever-- and-- and gutted it and modernized it.
And he was a friend of my father's-- friend, sort of an acquaintance of my father's.
And he said, why don't you take the upstairs, and you'll give me an amount for building expenses, and I can arrange this upstairs to your liking and to your needs.
And so my father and the architect drew the lines where this room would be and that room and where the bathroom and so on and so forth.
I mean, certain things were a given, but others could be switched around and the modern bathrooms put in an so forth.
And this is how come we lived up there.
But this gentlemen was married to a Gentile [INAUDIBLE] lady.
So when Hitler came, he was able to get to England, and she was the landlady now.
That's how they also could maintain the property.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she was a very tough cookie.

And, as I said, I mean, this was one flight up and below was the concierge, the caretaker-- the couple that saw to it that the heat was on and the hot water was running and the stairs were clean, and so forth.
And when she engaged that couple, she said, no children.
I can't stand it.
I can't tolerate it-- no children.
That was a condition.
Now, there was a lot of unemployment.
I told you about the social circumstances.
When I was a young person, there was a lot of discontent and unrest and poverty, and that, of course, helped dictate a good common promise, whatever.
When Hitler came, this couple said, now we are going to get even with her, went to the party, and said, she is listening to foreign broadcasts.
This was punishable by [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] death and then some, I suppose, because by then, the war had broken out.
Don't forget-- '39, the war broke out in Europe.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By then, of course, we didn't live there anymore because after my father left, when he was released, he had to leave within 30 days.

He managed in the 30 days to get all our paintings, get the permission and the license and-- and the paintings packed in a lift van and the furniture, and my trousseau, and so on, so on, and so forth.
And the lift van was sealed, ready for transportation to the United States as soon as we were going to leave.
So we had no money to pay storage in the United States.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we got the card, my mother and I, after my father left, that within 24 hours, we had to leave the place.

And then we were sent to a so-called Jew house, and we didn't have much to move because the furniture was gone already, and much of our clothes was in the lift van because we were supposed to leave on September the sixth, mother and I.
Father left on August 18th.
He got a-- a visa to go to England until he can go to the United States, which he thought would be two, three weeks.
And mother and I would then join.
Mother had the power of attorney and was going to close up and wait for the quota to be due on September the sixth.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we had very little when we moved to the Jew house.
Of course, the war broke out on the second, and we never came to the United States on the sixth.
That's another long story.
We were there until June '41.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I was leading up to something-- yes.
So we were living in the Jew house, and two SS-- that's the black uniform.
That's one notch more scary than the brown.
The black uniform came up.
And my mother was at her sister's, and I was home alone.
And they said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Ja?
Mitkommen.
Come along.
They didn't ask whether I was the one with the power of attorney or not.
They took me.
What they really wanted was my mother, who had the power of attorney.
And they wanted to know what's in the lift van, and did we smuggle some gold out and whatnot.
And they came from what was called [SPEAKING GERMAN], the agency concerned with smuggling and goods crossing the border and whatnot.
Very fortunately, my mother was not at home, because she was not up to these things.
I was young and strong and healthy and gutsy.
Anyway, I went with them to the office of the [SPEAKING GERMAN], but they were very busy there, so they put me down in the waiting room.
And by then it got late and the-- the official there had no time for an interview, whatever they intended to do.
So they put me in a broom closet in the meantime.
And there I was in the broom closet, locked in, and I was just standing there and wondering, what next?
And you saw the lights through the crack in the door and heard the-- the footsteps and nothing else to do but to listen.
You can't plan.
There's nothing to plan.
You can't think.
There's this-- I couldn't.

And so my amusement was that these footsteps were the footsteps of these SS people and the footsteps of the secretaries, clip, clop with their high heels-- kind of a musical entertainment, very funny.
And then the lights dimmed, and the [IMITATING FOOTSTEPS] left.
And the clip clop ended.
And I was locked in the broom closet.
And I started to holler and to scream, and somebody came, unlocked the broom closet, and was extremely surprised to find a person among the brooms and didn't know what to do with that person.
By then, it was maybe 6:00.
That was early spring, which is pretty cold, and I was still wearing a coat and the day's are not very long, so it was dusk outside.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
So called some-- some party official.

And they put me in-- in a-- in a private car-- it was not the police or anything-- and took me to the police [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] holding place-- which had 100 years ago, or till the end of the monarchy, been the barracks for the regiment, for the Vienna regiment.
It was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] barracks, large barracks, [SPEAKING GERMAN] on the Donaukanal-- red brick, big towers, very impressive.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was taken there and this person who brought me, because they didn't know what to do with me, just put me down there at the-- at the entrance, at the officer's desk and took off and didn't say anything.
Obviously, there was no file.
There was no nothing.
Nobody knew whom they had or why they had her.
So they took my belt.
I was wearing a suit with a belt.
And they took my hat and my gloves.
But they left everything else.
And nothing was said.
Nothing was explained, or-- nothing nice or ugly happened.
And then somebody opened a cell door and pushed me in.
This happen to be a single cell, and there were four or five people in it already.
And since by then it was dark, and it was close to 7:00, they had had their evening meal, and they had gotten ready for the night.
Now, since it was a single cell, and it was so overcrowded, they slept on straw mattresses on the floor, which means that the entire floor was covered.
There was only one bed in that single cell, which was sort of flipped up towards the wall, and that was flipped down, and that was given to the oldest person.
And the oldest person was a Jehovah's Witness.
And over her bed was a shelf.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So here I am, being pushed in and no place to go.
I mean, they pushed me in the door, and I was standing at the door because I couldn't take a step.
Also, it was dusk.
I didn't see.
And there I was standing, and this woman on the bed slowly reaches up to the shelf, puts in her teeth and says, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are you Jewish?
And I say, yes.
And these women on the floor got very, very active, very busy, very excited, because they were not going to have a Jew person there.
And the woman on the shelf-- on that bed-- said-- the Jehovah's Witness said-- let her be.
They are also human.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One more time when my life was saved.
That saved my life.
This woman said I was human, so I was let be.
There was only one problem.
Where am I going to be?
Where am I going to spend the night?
Now, in the corner was an open john-- no seat, no cover, no nothing, just-- and I could be along-- since it was open, I couldn't even sit on it.
But I could slide alongside at the wall with my knees thrown up.
And that's where I spent the night.
Except if somebody had to use the john, they would yank up their skirt and go to the john.
The skirt would fall over me.
[LAUGHS] And I don't know what's going to happen.
I had no idea.
I was worried about my mother.
Will she ever find out where I am and get over it, I mean-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
